coredata.dc:title: A chemiluminescent probe for highly sensitive detection of trifluralin based on cobalt ion-complexed boron nitride quantum dots as efficient nanocatalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Shokri, Roghayeh
Amjadi, Mohammad
Manzoori, Jamshid L.
coredata.dc:description: Trifluralin is a hazardous herbicide and its residual in the environment has irreversible effects for humans and other organisms, so its environmental monitoring is of particular importance. In this work, a highly sensitive chemiluminescence (CL)-based probe was developed for this purpose. Boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs), complexed with Co2+ ions were used as efficient CL nanocatalysts for the reaction of pyrogallol with H2O2. It was found that BNQD-Co2+ can amplify the CL signal of this reaction as great as 2000 times, while the enhancement factor with BNQDs and Co2+ alone was 40 and 25, respectively. This effect was attributed to the formation of the BNQD-Co2+ complex with tremendous catalytic action on the oxidation of pyrogallol by H2O2. In the presence of trifluralin, because of its possible optical and chemical interactions with the probe, the CL signal considerably decreased. Therefore, the enhanced CL system was exploited as a sensitive probe to determine trifluralin. The linear range of the probe for trifluralin was 20 nM − 90 µM, with a detection limit of 6.0 nM. It was employed for the interference-free and accurate analysis of water and soil samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Luminescence
Chemosensor
Pesticide
Environmental analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Establishment of a chemosensor for TFA
Determination of TFA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) 3-D fluorescence emission spectra varying from 360 to 510 nm wavelengths for BNQDs excited at 270–330 nm wavelength range. b) UV–Vis spectrum of BNQDs, Inset: photographs of BNQDs under the visible light (left) and UV lamp at 362 nm (right).
a) TEM image, b) FT-IR spectrum, c) EDX spectrum, and d) XRD pattern of BNQDs.

Kinetic curves (at the optimum conditions) of H2O2– pyrogallol (blue line-a), H2O2– pyrogallol –Co2+ (purple line-b), H2O2– pyrogallol -BNQDs (orange line-c), and H2O2– pyrogallol -BNQDs-Co2+ (green line-d) CL reactions.
CL spectrum of H2O2– pyrogallol -BNQDs-Co2+ obtained by continuous flow of reagents (pyrogallol (0.01 M), BNQDs and Co2+ (0.1 M) solutions in one line, and H2O2 (1 M) solution in other line).

UV–Vis spectra of H2O2– pyrogallol (blue line-a), H2O2– pyrogallol –Co2+ (purple line-b), H2O2– pyrogallol -BNQDs (orange line-c), and H2O2– pyrogallol -BNQDs-Co2+ (green line-d); H2O2 (20 mM), pyrogallol (30 µM), BNQDs (30-fold diluted) and Co2+ (0.3 mM).). Inset: photographs of solutions (from left to right, H2O2– pyrogallol, H2O2– pyrogallol –BNQDs, H2O2– pyrogallol –Co2+ and H2O2– pyrogallol -BNQDs-Co2+.
Calibration curve and corresponding CL signals for TFA.
Comparison of the methods for determination of TFA.
Tolerance limits of some potentially interfering species in the determination of 0.1 µM TFA under optimum conditions.

row.entry: Tolerance limit (interfering species to analyte concentration ratio)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of TFA in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aThe concentration unit for water samples is µM and for soil samples is mg Kg−1. bMean of three determinations ± standard deviation ct-Critical = 4.3 for n = 2, P = 0.05.

head.ce:title: A chemiluminescent probe for highly sensitive detection of trifluralin based on cobalt ion-complexed boron nitride quantum dots as efficient nanocatalysts

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trifluralin is a hazardous herbicide and its residual in the environment has irreversible effects for humans and other organisms, so its environmental monitoring is of particular importance. In this work, a highly sensitive chemiluminescence (CL)-based probe was developed for this purpose. Boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs), complexed with Co2+ ions were used as efficient CL nanocatalysts for the reaction of pyrogallol with H2O2. It was found that BNQD-Co2+ can amplify the CL signal of this reaction as great as 2000 times, while the enhancement factor with BNQDs and Co2+ alone was 40 and 25, respectively. This effect was attributed to the formation of the BNQD-Co2+ complex with tremendous catalytic action on the oxidation of pyrogallol by H2O2. In the presence of trifluralin, because of its possible optical and chemical interactions with the probe, the CL signal considerably decreased. Therefore, the enhanced CL system was exploited as a sensitive probe to determine trifluralin. The linear range of the probe for trifluralin was 20 nM − 90 µM, with a detection limit of 6.0 nM. It was employed for the interference-free and accurate analysis of water and soil samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Analytical assays based on chemiluminescence (CL) have many advantages such as high sensitivity, extensive dynamic range, simplicity of operation, affordable equipment and rapid response, which make them suitable choices for many applications [1,2]. Recently, quantum dots (QDs) have been extensively utilized as enhancers in many CL systems [3]. Various QDs have different influences on the CL, because of varying morphology, size and surface functional groups and optical properties [4]. The toxicity of heavy-metal QDs raises environmental and human health concerns and limits their applications. Boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs), a new member of green QDs, do not have this limitation [5] and have other attractive advantages such as good dispersibility, biocompatibility, chemical stability, wide bandgap and easy synthesis procedures. Although the capability of BNQDs as a photoluminescence probe [5–11] and electrochemiluminescence co-reactant [12–16] has been studied, to the best of our knowledge, the effect of BNQDs in the CL systems has not previously been reported.

Pesticides are extensively used in agriculture for increasing productivity and improving the quality of agricultural products. Among various pesticides, herbicides are the most widely used globally because of their function in controlling and changing the phytosociological composition of weeds without adverse effect on the yield of the products [17]. Trifluralin (TFA, Fig. S1 (ESM)) is a universal dinitroaniline herbicide that is inexpensive and effective, applied for weeding approximately all kinds of large-scale crop cultivations, like cotton, sugarcane, sunflowers and soybean [18]. It is estimated that more than 4400 tons of TFA are used globally per year though it is ranked in the top five most hazardous pesticides [19]. It is a serious threat to human health and the environment because of its mutagenicity and endocrine-disrupting effect causes cancer and also hurts to the liver, kidney, adrenal gland, muscle and skin [20,21] Additionally the fatal doses of it for macrofauna, microfauna and especially aquatic organisms is as low as 0.2 µM [19]. TFA is relatively stable and pollute soils, groundwater and rivers for a long time [22]. Therefore, detection of TFA in environmental samples is of great importance, and various analytical methods such as chromatography [23–25], electrochemical [26–29] and fluorescence [21,22,30–32], have been applied for this purpose. CL is one of the most desirable methods for sensitive assay of pesticides. Various CL systems have been used for detection of pesticides [33]. Recently CuInS2QDs-KMnO4 [34], luminol-H2O2 [35], rhodamine B-H2O2 [36], and AuNPs- peroxyoxalate [37] CL systems have been used for detection of diniconazole, triazophos, organophosphates, and thiram, respectively. However, to the best of our knowledge, no CL method has been reported for TFA assay.

Herein, we report on a sensitive CL method for TFA quantification, which is based on the use of BNQDs. The mixture of BNQDs and Co2+ was utilized to obtain a unique amplified CL signal from the H2O2– pyrogallol system. Investigations showed that TFA has a suppressing effect on the amplified CL signal and based on this fact, a sensitive and selective chemosensor was introduced for TFA.

Monitoring of CL signal, recording UV–Vis spectra, and the CL and fluorescence spectra were performed by a LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer with an automatic injector (Berthold, Germany), a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Sydney, Australia), JASCO FP-8300 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan) equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cell, respectively. In order to characterize the prepared BNQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Philips EM208S 100KV), FT-IR spectrometry (Bruker Co., Germany), energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) (MIRA III, France), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Siemens D5000, Germany) were exploited.

A gas chromatograph (GC) (Agilent 6890 N) /mass spectrometer (MS) (Agilent 5973) was exploited to measure TFA in real samples. The specifications of Capillary Column were as follows: Agilent 19091S-433, HP-5MS, 0.25 mm diameter × 30 m length × 0.25 µm film thickness. The temperature programming was as follows: 40 °C initial temperature (held 3 min), ramped at 15 ◦C min−1 until 130 ◦C, 10 ◦C min−1 until 270 ◦C, 20 ◦C min−1 until 300 ◦C, and held at 300 ◦C for 10 min [38].

Boric acid, urea, hydrogen peroxide, cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Pyrogallol (1,2,3-trihydroxy-benzol) and trifluralin were received from Sigma-Aldrich. Ultra-pure water (aqua MAX - Ultra 370 water purification system, Young Lin Instrument Co., Korea) was used throughout the experiment. The hydrogen peroxide (1.0 M) and pyrogallol (1.0 mM) stock solutions were prepared daily. A 1.0 mM TFA stock solution was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of this substance in a minimum volume of methanol and reached 50 mL with deionized water and stored in the dark at 4 °C.

For synthesis of BNQDs, 0.045 g boric acid and 0.075 g urea as B and N sources, respectively, were dissolved in 15 mL deionized water. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 210 °C for 20 h. Then, the solution was cooled to room temperature [39]. The obtained BNQD solution was purified by dialysis (MW = 2000) against deionized water for 4 h, and stored for experiments.

For CL measurement, 100 µL of pyrogallol solution (1.0 mM), 400 µL of BNQD and 50 µL of CoCl2 (0.01 M) solution were pipetted into 3-mL cuvette. Then, a suitable volume of TFA standard or sample was added and the volume was reached 950 μL with deionized water. In the last step, 50 μL of H2O2 solution (1.0 M) was injected by the automatic injector and the CL signal was continuously monitored. Maximum intensity was used as an analytical signal.

Well water and soil samples were collected from two different farms near Tabriz city (Iran). Soil samples were dried at room temperature and sieved through a metal sieve (100 meshes), and spiked with appropriate amounts of TFA. 2.0 g of each sample was mixed with 10 mL of acetone and after shaking overnight, it was filtered. 2.0 mL of the collected acetone extracts was transferred to a test tube and the acetone was evaporated by a stream of nitrogen gas. The residue was dissolved in 1.0 mL of methanol and used to determine TFA [40,41].

Before any pretreatment, the water samples were spiked with appropriate amounts of TFA standard solution for recovery experiments and then filtered using a filter paper (ALBET, DP 135 125, Barcelona, Spain) to remove the particulate materials. Finally, the proper amount of the solution was analyzed according to the general procedure.

The 3-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of BNQDs is shown in Fig. 1 a. The excitation wavelength varied from 255 to 400 nm and fluorescence emission was recorded in the wavelength range of 300 to 500 nm. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 295 nm and 395 nm, respectively and as seen, at higher excitation wavelengths, BNQDs show excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence. The fluorescence quantum yield of BNQDs was estimated to be 8.3% ± 1.5 using quinine sulfate as a standard.

Considering the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1b), BNQDs exhibit an absorption peak around 250 nm which corresponds to the bandgap energy for hexagonal BN [42]. The appearance of a broad shoulder around 300 nm may be due to the surface chemistry and edge defects [38]. Inset of Fig. 1b displays the blue fluorescence of colorless solution of BNQDs under the 362 nm UV light.

The TEM image together with size distribution of BNQDs is shown in Fig. 2 a, indicating that the prepared QDs have the average diameter of 6 nm. FTIR spectroscopy was conducted to recognize the chemical composition and functional groups of the BNQDs (Fig. 2b). The peaks at 1340, 1410 and 1627 cm−1 correspond to the B-N bond vibrations. The broad absorption peak at 3533 cm−1 was attributed to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The EDS spectrum of BNQDs (Fig. 2c) revealed the weight percent of boron, nitrogen, carbon and oxygen to be 28.4, 10.2, 14.1 and 47.3%, respectively. The appearance of C and O in prepared BNQDs is expected, because of the existence of C and O in the raw materials and contamination from the carbon film used in EDS measurement [10]. In XRD pattern of BNQDs (Fig. 2d) a typical weak and broad peak at 2θ = ∼26° appeared which indicates that BNQDs have a thin and low-crystalline structure without interlayer interactions [11,43].

A few probes based on H2O2– pyrogallol CL system have been reported [44]. This system exhibits a very weak and slow CL signal as shown in curve a, in Fig. 3 . Interestingly, by addition of both BNQDs and Co2+ to this system, the CL signal is greatly enhanced (2000- fold) and the reaction becomes much faster (curve d, Fig. 3). However, when only BNQDs is added to H2O2– pyrogallol system, the CL signal is enhanced about 40-fold and the maximum CL reaches within 3 s after H2O2 injection, (as seen in curve c in Fig. 3). Also, in the presence of only Co2+, the CL signal is enhanced about 25- fold and reaches to the maximum with much slower rate (curve b, Fig. 3).

It should be mentioned that, the effect of components utilized in the preparation of BNQDs (boric acid and urea) on the CL system was also investigated and observed that they do not affect the CL signal.

Similar behavior has previously been reported for the complex of BNQDs with Cu2+ in the oxidation of dopamine [9]. It is interesting to mention that BNQD-Cu2+ exhibited much lower enhancing effect (800 times) than BNQD-Co2+ on the H2O2-pyrogallol reaction.

The efficacy of radical scavengers on the CL signal of the H2O2– pyrogallol-BNQD-Co2+ system was also checked. The results were as follows, thiourea (8.0 mM), ascorbic acid (0.01 M) and sodium azide (0.01 M) quench the CL intensity 64%, 48% and 32%, respectively. Additionally, the CL was measured after deoxygenation of the solution. It was realized that the CL signal diminishes 28% after removing dissolved oxygen. These results ascertain the critical role of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the CL reaction of H2O2– pyrogallol-BNQD-Co2+, which ultimately leads to the generation of (1O2*)2. Overall, the BNQD-Co2+ complex might be able to catalyze the generation of PP as well as the production of (1O2*)2, which leads to an incredible CL signal enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of a chemosensor for TFA
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary investigations indicated that trace amounts of TFA impressively reduce the CL signal of the H2O2-pyrogallol-BNQD-Co2+ system. Since TFA has been prepared in methanol, the effect of this solvent was tested; it has no significant effect on the CL signal. As shown in Fig. S4 (ESM), TFA has a broad absorption peak spanning the wavelength range of 200 to 550 nm. This well overlaps with the emission spectrum of PP*. So inner filter effect can be considered the most probable mechanism for CL diminishing. However, the interaction of TFA with BNQDs may also contribute to the phenomenon. The electron-withdrawing character of nitro groups in the structure of TFA, may lead to its attachment to the electron-rich surface groups of BNQDs, thereby altering the catalytic activity of the BNQD-Co2+ complex. The quenching of fluorescence of BNQDs by TFA (Fig. S5-a) indicates the existence of an interaction between BNQDs and TFA. It should be mentioned that this quenching is not solely due to inner filter effect of TFA since after correction of this effect with a suitable correction factor [49], the quenching still is present (Fig S5-b).

The response of H2O2-pyrogallol-BNQD-Co2+ probe for some pesticides are shown in Fig. S7. As can be seen it exhibited the highest sensitivity for TFA. Moreover, the selectivity of the method for TFA determination was investigated by monitoring the CL response of the probe for 0.1 µM TFA in the presence of some other pesticides, phenolic compounds and common inorganic ions. The tolerable concentration ratios for interferences in the relative error of < 5% are summarized in Table 2 . These results show that the method possesses a good selectivity for analyzing real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TFA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The potential usefulness of the CL probe for the detection of TFA in soil and water samples was studied. The obtained results are given in Table 3 . As can be seen, the Student t-test shows that there are no significant differences between added and found values. The accuracy of the method was further tested by comparing the results with those obtained by the GC–MS method. The GC–MS chromatograms are reported in Fig. S8 (ESM). Again, statistical analysis proves that there are no significant differences between the results of the two methods. Therefore, the developed method exhibits promising applicability for reliable and accurate analysis of TFA in real samples.

In summary, we showed that BNQD forms a complex with Co2+ ion, which exerts tremendous catalytic effect on the oxidation reaction of pyrogallol with H2O2, leading to an extraordinary enhancement of its CL intensity (2000 times). BNQDs or Co2+ alone did not have such high activity and their enhancement effect on the reaction was much lower. Therefore, this study introduced a new nanocatalyst with a unique behavior in CL. The H2O2-pyrogallol-BNQD-Co2+ CL system was then utilized for establishing a sensitive assay for TFA. The method is sensitive and selective for TFA and has a good accuracy for real sample analysis as proved by GC–MS results.

Roghayeh Shokri: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mohammad Amjadi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Jamshid L. Manzoori: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107759.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on applications of chemiluminescence detection in food analysis
Anal. Chim. Acta

Trends Anal. Chem.
